channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: U.S. Military Says Iran Targeted American Forces in Kuwait
item.description: The U.S. military intercepted two Iranian missiles early Monday and no American personnel were harmed, Central Command said. The attacks threatened to further complicate talks to end the war.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: How Lebanon’s Best Chance to Disarm Hezbollah Failed
item.description: Lebanon’s government has long wanted the powerful militia to give up its weapons. Before the Iran war began, there were signs of progress toward that goal.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Aaron Boxerman
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:credit: David Guttenfelder/The New York Times
item.media:description: Climbing through rubble in central Beirut, Lebanon, after an Israeli airstrike in March.

item.title: Oil Prices Jump as U.S. and Iran Exchange Fire
item.description: Oil prices climbed as investors weighed a renewed exchange of military strikes between the United States and Iran against signs that both sides remain engaged in negotiations.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images

item.title: U.S. Military Is Quietly Guiding Ships Through the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: U.S. Central Command has helped around 70 commercial ships pass through the strait in the last three weeks, an official said.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Vessels waiting to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. Before the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran, well over 100 commercial ships a day passed through the strait.

item.title: Inside an Ebola Ward, and a Roadblock for Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund
item.description: Plus, the 20-year-old director with the No. 1 movie.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and Declan Walsh
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Taking lab supplies into the sun for reuse at a hospital that is treating Ebola patients in Ituri Province, Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: They Voted for Trump. Here’s How They Feel About High Gas Prices.
item.description: With the midterm elections five months away, many Americans who backed President Trump are wrestling with pain at the pump and the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk, Rachel Richardson and Sam Easter
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kevin Mohatt/Reuters

item.title: Ex-F.B.I. Officials Form New Group to Help Agents Grapple With Patel’s Changes
item.description: The F.B.I. Support Network offers legal, mental health and job search services to current agency employees. Its founders say the work force is incredibly strained under Kash Patel.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Seth Wenig/Associated Press
item.media:description: Among the members of the new group is Brian Driscoll, who briefly became the acting F.BI. director in early 2025, only to be fired in August after repeatedly clashing with the Trump administration.

item.title: Are Texans Ready for Talarico’s Kind of Christianity?
item.description: Jim Rigby, a pastor who rarely uses the word “God,” is a key to understanding the Senate candidate trying to pull off something unusual in Texas.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Graham and J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jim Rigby has led St. Andrew’s Presbyterian Church in Austin, Texas, since the 1980s.

item.title: As Democrats Worry About Senate Race, Platner Attacks Reports About Sexual Messages
item.description: Graham Platner, whose contest in Maine is a key to Democrats’ hopes of winning the Senate, sought to discredit reports that he had exchanged sexual messages with women outside his marriage.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Tim Balk and Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner, a Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine, is trying to unseat Senator Susan Collins, a Republican.

item.title: What to Watch For in the NY Primary: Key Races and Why They Matter
item.description: Congressional primaries in Manhattan, Brooklyn and beyond could test the strength of Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s socialist movement and reverberate across the country.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Competitive primary contests are taking place in six House districts in New York City and elsewhere in the state.

item.title: A Paralympic Champion and an Eagle Scout Give Democrats Hope in Deep-Red Iowa
item.description: Tuesday’s Senate primary features two candidates with compelling personal stories. Both have stressed their independence.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kruse
item.media:credit: Zach Boyden-Holmes/The Register, via Imagn Images

item.title: The Return of Blaming and Shaming in Public Health
item.description: For years, medicine has tried to eliminate stigma. Now, Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is bringing back the language of personal responsibility.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Claire Merchlinsky/The New York Times; Photographs by Getty

item.title: Sam Levinson on Why ‘Euphoria’ Had to End This Way. For Good.
item.description: In an exclusive interview, Sam Levinson explained why he felt it was time to bring the series to a close and responded to its detractors.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Caroline Tompkins

item.title: On ‘Euphoria’ and Elsewhere, TV Is Having a Sex Cam Spring
item.description: Virtual sex work is nothing new on TV. But this year it is more visible, central to series like “Euphoria,” “Margo’s Got Money Troubles” and “Maximum Pleasure Guaranteed.”
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski

item.title: China Aims A.I. at Predicting Who Could Pose a Political Risk
item.description: New research examines how a Chinese company struggled to develop its predictive surveillance technology while U.S. restrictions were in place.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: The skyline of Beijing. A Chinese firm is working on products that use A.I. to examine location data and internet use to predict who could do or say something critical of the government.

item.title: How an Act of Vandalism Helped a Dissident Find His Voice
item.description: Li Ying, known as Teacher Li to his 2.2 million followers on X, doesn’t live in China but still faces smear campaigns and death threats. He is not letting that stop him.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations

item.title: What It’s Like to Be a Student at the First A.I.-Powered University
item.description: California’s public university system spent $16.9 million on A.I. The result has been chaos.
item.dc:creator: Linda Kinstler

item.title: Prized by ‘MAHA’ Influencers and Chefs Alike, Craft Flour Is on the Rise
item.description: Growing demand for fiber-rich grains is boosting the premium flour industry, which offers less processed, but pricier, alternatives to the classic grocery store brands.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Kronsberg
item.media:credit: Amy Lombard for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin Morse, left, C.E.O. and co-founder of Cairnspring Mills, with Bryan Ford at Ford’s Brooklyn bakery, Diljān.

item.title: Jeffrey Epstein’s Sperm May Have Survived Him
item.description: Mr. Epstein banked his sperm several years before his death and said that if he died, it should be left in the control of his estate.
item.dc:creator: Jacqueline Mroz and Maggie Astor
item.media:credit: Claire Merchlinsky/The New York Times; Photographs by Getty

item.title: Starmer Braces for More Files About Mandelson, Ex-Diplomat With Ties to Epstein
item.description: A second batch of documents about the former U.S. ambassador Peter Mandelson, who was fired over his links to Jeffrey Epstein, could create more problems for Prime Minister Keir Starmer.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Toby Melville/Reuters
item.media:description: Peter Mandelson, a former British ambassador to the United States, in London in April. He was fired from the post in September over his links to the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: Colombia Presidential Election Heads to a Runoff
item.description: The candidate, Abelardo de la Espriella, will face a senator from the left-wing party of the outgoing president, Gustavo Petro, in a June runoff.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal, Genevieve Glatsky and Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.media:credit: Sergio Acero/Reuters
item.media:description: Presidential candidate Abelardo De La Espriella addressing supporters in Barranquilla, Colombia, on Sunday.

item.title: N.Y.C. Children May Get Up to $3,000 From City for College Accounts
item.description: The city’s current savings plan starts with $100. A City Council proposal would make the initial contributions among the highest anywhere.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Haag
item.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
item.media:description: Julie Menin, the speaker of the City Council, is proposing a substantial expansion of the city’s investment in college savings plans.

item.title: Marco Rubio: Florida Centrist, Tea Party Darling, MAGA Warrior, President?
item.description: The secret to the Secretary of State’s success.
item.dc:creator: Manuel Roig-Franzia

item.title: Bernie Sanders: A.I. Belongs to the People, Not to Billionaires
item.description: All Americans should have a stake in the future of this technology.
item.dc:creator: Bernie Sanders

item.title: Death from Above
item.description: We look at U.S. boat strikes in the Caribbean Sea and eastern Pacific Ocean.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: The New York Times
item.media:description: Images from social media posts by President Trump, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and the U.S. Southern Command.

item.title: Nvidia Has a Plan to Put Its Chips in Personal Computers
item.description: The world’s most valuable company is chasing Intel and Apple as it tries to bring A.I. agents to laptops and desktops.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: I-Hwa Cheng/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Nvidia’s chief executive, Jensen Huang, in Taipei, Taiwan, last week. Nvidia is betting its computers will hit the market at the right time, just as A.I. agents are becoming more popular.

item.title: Syrian Officers Who Hid in Plain Sight Face War Crime Charges in Austria
item.description: The trial, Austria’s first against Assad regime officials, will be a rare chance for Syrian witnesses to confront two men they have accused of torture.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
Commission for International Justice and Accountability
item.media:credit: Joe Klamar/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Khaled al-Halabi, a Syrian former brigadier general, entering court in Vienna on Monday. He is the highest-ranking official of the Assad regime to appear in a European court on charges of war crimes.

item.title: Notre Dame Students Abused by Priest in ‘Weighing Scheme,’ Report Finds
item.description: An investigation commissioned by Notre Dame found that the Catholic university could have done more to respond to allegations of sexual misconduct.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Saul
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: A new report details allegations of sexual abuse at the University of Notre Dame.
